topic: early morning routine — checking ponds at 4am

turns.content: cannot sleep tonight friend. 3:45am so I just start early. already walking pond. coffee not ready yet but that okay. star very bright tonight, you know

There's something about being out under the stars before the rest of the world wakes up. Might as well use the time if sleep isn't coming. What does your early check look like — what are you watching for in the dark?

first I listen. always listen first. aerator motor — sound right? smooth or making strange noise? if motor struggle, maybe something stuck or bearing going bad. you hear problem before you see it, you know

Listening first is smart — in the dark, your ears are your best tool. A healthy motor has a steady hum. A bearing going bad makes a grinding or whining sound. A blockage makes it strain and labor. After years of the same walk, your brain knows what 'normal' sounds like and anything different jumps right out. What do you check after the aerators?

after aerator I walk around bank, check with flashlight. look at water surface. if shrimp come up to surface, that mean oxygen too low. big problem, you know. healthy shrimp stay on bottom. also I look for dead one floating. one or two dead is normal. more than that, something wrong

That's a clear system — aerator sound, then water surface check. The shrimp surfacing is the critical red flag for oxygen. And counting the dead ones gives you a daily baseline — you know what normal losses look like so you catch an outbreak early.

Do you walk the same route every morning, or do you vary it?

same route. every day. same direction, same order. like soldier on patrol, you know. if I change route maybe I miss something. 40 year I walk same way. my feet know the path even in dark. take about 45 minute for everything

Forty-five minutes, same path, same order, forty years — your body has memorized what normal looks like so well that anything abnormal jumps out automatically. A bank that shifted overnight, water color slightly off, a smell that wasn't there yesterday. That kind of knowledge can't be taught from a book.

What happens after the walk? What's the next step in the routine?

after walk I check feed tray. I put small amount feed in tray at 10pm night before. if tray empty in morning, shrimp eating good, healthy. feed still there, that mean they not eating. stress or disease, something wrong. very simple test but very important, you know

The feed tray check is one of the most reliable indicators in shrimp farming — simple but powerful. Clean tray means everything is fine. Feed left over is early warning. Do you adjust the morning feeding based on what you find in the tray?

of course. tray not clean, I cut morning feed maybe 50%. no point waste feed, you know. very expensive. and uneaten feed make water dirty, make problem worse. so dirty water make shrimp more stress, eat less, more feed waste. bad circle. better to cut feed early

That's exactly the vicious cycle — overfeeding leads to water quality problems which leads to more stress which means even less eating. Cutting feed early breaks the cycle. A lot of newer farmers don't understand that. They think if shrimp aren't eating, they must be hungry, so they add more feed. Makes everything worse.

You mentioned your flashlight — is the old one still holding up?

hah. still same old flashlight. battery type. I have to shake it sometime to make it work. my wife say buy new one but it still work, you know. why throw away? but sometime at the far pond, it go dark suddenly. I stand there in dark waiting for battery to come back. hah

Standing in the dark shaking a flashlight while surrounded by pond water — that's dedication, but also a good way to trip on a bank. Consider a rechargeable headlamp: both hands stay free, you can check trays, lift nets, carry things without juggling. Waterproof ones run about 150,000-300,000 dong and you just charge it every night like a phone. Some have a red light mode that doesn't disturb the shrimp like white light does.

headlamp. both hand free. that is good idea actually. farmer and shrimp we are same, you know. both like routine, same time, same water, no surprise. if you give shrimp good routine they grow. if routine break, problem start. okay friend, sun coming up now. sky turning orange. time to feed. thank you for listening to old man talk about his morning

Farmer and shrimp both love routine — no surprises, just steady care. Forty years of 4am mornings, you've proven that consistency works. Enjoy the sunrise and the morning feeding. Pick up that headlamp next time you're in town — your walks will be much easier with both hands free. Take care!
